quick_facts.label: Core public-health function
quick_facts.value: Epidemiologists use surveillance and other health data to investigate outbreaks, guide response, support disease-control efforts, and translate findings into policy-relevant conclusions.

quick_facts.label: MPH-linked entry route
quick_facts.value: For NCDC's June 2026 IDSP Consultant Epidemiologist terms of reference, an MPH is one of the accepted postgraduate qualifications when paired with an eligible bachelor’s background; requirements are vacancy-specific rather than a universal licence to practise.

quick_facts.label: Applied field-epidemiology pathway
quick_facts.value: NCDC’s India Epidemic Intelligence Service (EIS) is a two-year, mentor-based, training-through-service programme in surveillance, outbreak investigation and data analysis; participants can earn an integrated Master in Applied Epidemiology subject to university requirements.

quick_facts.label: Typical work setting
quick_facts.value: Government public-health programmes can place epidemiologists across national, state and district surveillance systems, with monitoring visits and frequent travel to assigned states or field sites.

quick_facts.label: Dated compensation reference
quick_facts.value: A June 2026 NCDC-IDSP contractual Consultant Epidemiologist notice stated a consolidated ₹90,000–₹150,000 per month, tied to post-qualification experience. This is a specific public-sector contract band, not a national salary benchmark or a current offer.

sections.heading: What epidemiologists do
paragraphs: In Indian public-health surveillance roles, epidemiologists organise and analyse surveillance data, monitor reporting and programme implementation, provide feedback to States and Union Territories, and convert evidence into scientifically sound conclusions that can inform policy.

They may initiate and guide outbreak investigations, support integration of disease-control activities using surveillance findings, coordinate with laboratories and programme partners, supervise and train state or district epidemiology staff, and prepare performance or technical reports.
bullets: Analyse, interpret and communicate surveillance and investigation data.
Investigate outbreaks using established procedures and coordinate response partners.
Strengthen surveillance and public-health programme implementation through monitoring, training and field support.
Prepare technical outputs such as reports, abstracts, presentations and policy-relevant findings.

sections.heading: Routes from an MPH into epidemiology
paragraphs: An MPH is a relevant immediate-parent qualification for this role, but entry criteria vary by employer, programme and seniority. In NCDC’s June 2026 IDSP Consultant Epidemiologist terms of reference, eligible applicants needed an accepted bachelor’s degree—such as MBBS, BDS, nursing, pharmacy, or specified quantitative, computing or life-science degrees—plus a postgraduate qualification including MPH, community medicine/PSM, epidemiology, biostatistics, health informatics or healthcare management.

Graduates seeking applied field training can consider NCDC’s India EIS route. It is a competitive two-year, mentor-based programme in which officers work with public-health agencies while building competence in surveillance, outbreak investigation, data analysis and scientific communication. However, its published eligibility is more restrictive than simply holding an MPH: examples include BDS or BVSc plus an MPH and three years’ relevant public-health experience, or specified medical qualifications and experience.

Other project and research posts may use different combinations of qualifications. ICMR recruitment examples list postgraduate epidemiology/public-health qualifications such as MAE, MPH, MSc or PhD as desirable alongside medical, research-methodology, health-system or subject-specific experience. Applicants should therefore read the precise notification for each post rather than assume one standard national eligibility rule.
bullets: Complete an MPH with strong epidemiology, biostatistics, research-methods and field-practice components.
Build demonstrable experience through surveillance, health-programme, research, registry, monitoring or outbreak-response work.
Apply to vacancy-specific government, research-institute, university, NGO or international-agency roles that match the degree and experience criteria.
For experienced public-health professionals, pursue structured field training such as NCDC India EIS where eligible.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: The strongest evidence-based skill set combines epidemiology with practical data work: disease-surveillance knowledge, epidemiologic study design, outbreak investigation, data analysis and the ability to communicate results clearly to technical and programme audiences.

For the 2026 NCDC-IDSP Consultant Epidemiologist role, advanced Excel capability was mandatory and working knowledge of SPSS, Stata or R was preferred. The notice also sought analytical ability, oral and written communication, computer proficiency and willingness to travel. NCDC field-epidemiology training similarly covers biostatistics, surveillance, outbreak response, laboratory roles in public health, risk communication and field practice.
bullets: Surveillance-data management, analysis and interpretation.
Study design, outbreak investigation and evaluation of surveillance systems.
Excel and at least one statistical package, such as R, Stata or SPSS.
Scientific writing, technical reporting, presentations and risk communication.
Teamwork, stakeholder coordination, training and readiness for field travel.

sections.heading: Where the work happens
paragraphs: Epidemiology work can be based in central, state and district public-health systems, including surveillance units, disease-control programmes and NCDC divisions. It commonly combines desk-based data analysis and technical reporting with health-facility, district and state visits.

Field placements in NCDC’s India EIS are typically within national and state health programmes, NCDC programme divisions, and state or district surveillance units. The programme describes rigorous fieldwork and travel at short notice; the IDSP consultant terms also require regular monitoring visits and frequent travel to allocated states.
bullets: Central and state government health programmes and surveillance units.
District public-health and health-system field sites.
Research institutes, registries and public-health research projects.
Academic, multilateral and non-governmental public-health organisations.

sections.heading: Career progression
paragraphs: A practical progression pattern is from MPH-supported analyst, project, surveillance or research work into increasingly independent field epidemiology, programme monitoring and technical-leadership responsibilities. Structured applied training can strengthen this transition: NCDC’s EIS requires field activities such as outbreak investigations, a planned epidemiological study and surveillance work, supported by supervisors and mentors.

NCDC reports that EIS alumni serve in public-health leadership or epidemiology roles in state and central government, academia, research institutions, municipal corporations, WHO, CDC and NGOs. Graduates are typically placed in state- and district-level public-health roles, while experienced professionals may later contribute as mentors, trainers or programme leads.
bullets: Early career: data collection and management, literature review, reporting, surveillance or research-project support.
Developing practitioner: independent analyses, outbreak or field investigations, training and supervision of operational staff.
Senior practitioner or leader: technical oversight, policy support, programme leadership, capacity building and mentoring.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Pay varies substantially by employer, contract type, location, qualifications, post-qualification experience and whether the post is government, research-project, academic, NGO or international-agency funded. It should be assessed from the live recruitment notice and its employment terms, not from a single advertised figure.

As a dated example, NCDC’s 30 June 2026 IDSP terms of reference advertised five contractual Consultant Epidemiologist posts with a ₹90,000–₹150,000 monthly consolidated-remuneration band: ₹90,000 for up to three years of post-qualification experience, ₹120,000 for more than three to under 10 years, and ₹150,000 for 10 or more years. The notice said the initial tenure ran to September 2026 and could be extended yearly; its application deadline was 30 June 2026. This past notice is useful only as role-specific compensation context.
bullets: Compare whether remuneration is consolidated or includes allowances, benefits and pension provisions.
Check the exact experience definition: some employers count only post-qualification experience.
Verify contract duration, renewal conditions, duty station and travel expectations before comparing offers.
